turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: Justice Sotomayor has the opinion on the Court this morning in case 15-777, Samsung Electronics versus Apple Incorporated.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: This case comes to us from the Court of Appeals for the Federal Circuit. It involves design patents specifically the way to calculate damages for design patent infringement. Section 289 of the Patent Act prohibits a person from manufacturing or selling an article of manufacture containing a patent design or close imitation of a patented design. It makes a person who violates that prohibition liable to the extent of his total profits from the article of manufacture. The design patents at issue in this case are held by Apple and cover aspects of its phone, iPhone, the familiar rectangular front face with rounded corners and the colorful grid of icons on the home screen. A jury found that various Samsung Smartphones infringed on those design patents. The infringement finding is not at issue here. What is at issue is the amount Samsung owes Apple as damages for the infringement. Before the Federal Circuit, Samsung argued that the total profit it owed to Apple was not the profit Samsung made on the Smartphones, here $399,000.000, instead Samsung argued that it owed only the profits it made on certain components of its infringing Smartphones, specifically the front face in the screen because those components were the articles of manufacture to which Samsung had applied Apple's patented designs. The Federal Circuit rejected Samsung's argument because Samsung did not sell those components separately to consumers. In its view, because Samsung sold the Smartphones as an integrated product, the components of those Smartphones were not articles of manufacture within the meaning of §289. We hold today that the term article of manufacture in §289 is not limited to things sold individually to consumers. The term is broad enough to embrace both products sold to a consumer and components of such products, whether sold separately or not. This reading is consistent with the plain meaning of the term article of manufacture. It is also consistent with how the same term has been understood in 35 U.S.C. §171(a), the provision that makes designs eligible for patent protection and with how the term manufacture has been understood in 35 U.S.C. §101, the provision that makes useful inventions eligible for patent protection. As a result, the Federal Circuit gave two narrow a meaning to the term article of manufacture in §289. We decline to go further and to resolve whether for each design patent at issue here. The article of manufacture is the Smartphone or a particular Smartphone component. Doing so would require devising a test to identify the relevant article of manufacture under §289, an issue Samsung and Apple did not brief and parsing the record to apply that test. Neither task is necessary to resolve the question presented and the federal circuit may address any remaining issues on remand. The judgment of the federal circuit is reversed and remanded for further proceedings. The decision of the Court is unanimous.
